restaurants.description: Thierry Voisin leads the main dining room here at the Imperial Hotel. True to its name, Les Saisons celebrates the finest ingredients of each season. Built on a classical French foundation and enriched with Japanese ingredients, the cuisine seeks harmony through sweetness, acidity and umami on every plate. The classical-modern setting offers a moment of timeless elegance. In keeping with the restaurant’s seasonal theme, the four private rooms are named Spring, Summer, Autumn and Winter.

restaurants.name: Les deux
restaurants.description: The name means ‘both’, and both the chef’s cuisine and the proprietress’s wine selections make guests feel at home. To showcase the seasonal ingredients’ flavours to the fullest, meals are served omakase-style only. Cocktails of snow crab, scallop and sea urchin make a flamboyant appetizer. In a welcome touch, portions are generous to ensure guests leave feeling full.The chef mixes his own sensibilities into cuisine rooted in French bistro fare. The blackboard menu is a medley of authentic dishes and à la carte items made with seasonal ingredients. Generally, an appetiser and a main course is the perfect amount, but some items can be ordered as half portions, so ask your server.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都目黒区上目黒2-7-2

restaurants.name: Les six
restaurants.description: The chef mixes his own sensibilities into cuisine rooted in French bistro fare. The blackboard menu is a medley of authentic dishes and à la carte items made with seasonal ingredients. Generally, an appetiser and a main course is the perfect amount, but some items can be ordered as half portions, so ask your server.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都港区南麻布5-15-25 2F

restaurants.name: Lien
restaurants.description: ‘Lien’ is French for ‘connection’. The chef, a native of Aomori Prefecture, uses both ingredients and crockery from that region of northern Honshu. Serving salmon from the Tsugaru Strait and Aomori Shamorock chicken on Tsugaru Kanayama Yaki pottery, the chef connects guests with producers through his cuisine. The comprehensive prix fixe menu is imbued with this sense of human connection. ‘Lien’ may mean a feeling of connection, but it also means a bond.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都世田谷区池尻3-5-22

restaurants.name: Loiseau de France
restaurants.description: Inside Institut Français de Tokyo, time flows slowly, the bustle of the city forgotten. The chalk-white building, designed to suggest a small village in the south of France, gently enfolds a courtyard. Poached eggs in red wine sauce and beef simmered in red wine bring a taste of Saulieu, in Burgundy’s Côte d’Or. Desserts are classic sweets selected from a trolley, as tradition dictates.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都新宿区市谷船河原町15

restaurants.name: Los Reyes Magos
restaurants.description: The second-generation chef passes on the Catalonian cuisine that the first-generation chef acquired in Barcelona. Salad of sardines and egg like grandma used to make honours the memory of this couple’s mentors. Dried cod and parsley sauce and homemade sausages convey the flavours of Catalonia, while the well-worn pots attest to the popularity of the paella. Cuisine interwoven with warm memories echoes the journey this restaurant has traced.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都渋谷区代々木5-55-7

restaurants.name: Lustre
restaurants.description: The guiding theme here is ‘Umami French’. Drawing on the five flavours of Japanese cuisine, French cooking is fused with the umami of Japanese ingredients. The foundations remain firmly classical, from technique to sauces. Kombu stock and fish sauce are added to the essences of vegetables, seafood and meat to draw out a powerful umami synergy. Named after the French word for chandelier, the restaurant shines a light on French cuisine shaped by Japanese sensibility.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都港区六本木7-13-10 B1F

restaurants.name: Lyla
restaurants.description: The name is an amalgam of the French words ‘lyrique’ and ‘laboratoire’. Leveraging his poetic creativity, the chef showcases the fruits of his research in his prix fixe offerings. The first half of the proceedings is steeped in playfulness, beginning with a balloon-shaped cocktail. In the second half, we get back to essentials, with fish and meat dishes dressed in sauces. Foodstuffs from Kyushu reflect the chef’s roots in Oita Prefecture. Be sure to try Oita’s famed kabosu citrus and its wines.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都港区赤坂7-5-34

restaurants.name: MAKIYAKI GINZA ONODERA
restaurants.description: A theatre-style open kitchen is centred around a wood-fired oven. Blazing flames flicker as the aroma of burning wood fills the room. All items on the prix fixe menu, from appetiser to dessert, are cooked over this wood fire. Seasonal ingredients from across Japan are prepared through grilling, steaming and smoking. A touch of smokiness and fragrant sauces lend each dish its distinctive character.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都中央区銀座5-14-14 9F

restaurants.name: MANOIR
restaurants.description: The restaurant has had a new chef since 2026. The interior, kitted out like an English manor house, is the reason for the name ‘Manoir’. Eager that guests should relax as if in their own homes, the owner/sommelier serves guests himself. The chef’s aim is to serve light French cuisine. The sweetness and tartness of fruit are overlaid with the umami of fermentation and salt-pickling. Game is delivered by hunters from Hokkaido and other places throughout Japan. Enjoy the wild flavours of game cuisine, with a glass of French wine.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都渋谷区広尾1-10-6

restaurants.name: MASIA
restaurants.description: To explore Japanese cuisine, the chef left his native Catalonia for Japan, where he opened a restaurant in Ginza to spread Catalan food culture in this country. His own roots, the Spanish soil and Japanese ingredients are the three keys to his fare. With the theme of ‘sea and mountain’, his prix fixe meals begin with tapas and proceed to arroz, the indispensable rice cuisine. Catalan home cooking gets a facelift with modern techniques.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都中央区銀座2-4-6 8F

restaurants.name: MATSUKI
restaurants.description: Enjoy pairings of local cooking with organic wines. The prix fixe opens with onion tart. Vegetable soup fragrant with basil brings memories of their apprenticeship in the south of France. Seeking warm, simple flavours, the chefs rely on time-honoured cooking utensils and techniques. Wearing aprons over everyday clothes, they chat casually with guests. An atmosphere that wants diners to enjoy French cuisine casually.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都中野区中野2-23-3

restaurants.name: MAZ
restaurants.address: 3F, Tokyo Garden Terrace Kioicho, 1-3 Kioicho, Chiyoda-ku, Tokyo, 102-0094, Japan
restaurants.description: This gustatory experience is a journey through Peru. The menu features the culinary traditions of the Andes and the ecosystem of the Amazon basin. Each menu item lists the producing region and, uniquely, its elevation. Taking these altitudes as a guide, the meal traces a route through sea, mountain and river, weaving a tale of diverse natural environments. Dried foods from Peru combine with Japanese ingredients to bring forth a cuisine that is richly imaginative.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都千代田区紀尾井町1-3 3F, Tokyo Garden Terrace Kioicho

restaurants.name: MIMOSA
restaurants.description: The counter seats are a uniform white, creating a sense of presence, and the sight of the cooking right in front of you is reassuring. Amid the sounds and aromas of stir-frying in woks, the chef sometimes makes conversation with guests while plating their food. Based on Shanghai cuisine, the dishes are set in front of you as soon as they are cooked. The set menu features numerous courses, the arrangements simple. Fresh ingredients express the seasons through Chinese gastronomy.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都港区南青山3-10-40 2F

restaurants.name: MONOLITH
restaurants.description: MONOLITH links the splendour of classic cuisine to the future. Pastry-wrapped meat symbolises this connection – the diner can choose from several types. Hewing close to time-honoured recipes, beef and lamb are dressed in a Madeira wine and truffle sauce, while pigeon is prepared in a rich salmis. Sauce is assiduously reduced and poured unstintingly, in true orthodox style. Respect for tradition and the classics, eloquently expressed through cooking.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都渋谷区渋谷2-6-1

restaurants.name: Ma Cuisine
restaurants.description: Ratatouille, cassoulet, beef cheek simmered in red wine: familiar regional fare covers the blackboard menu. Special attention is paid to Tokushima pork, which stars in numerous menu entries. Taking pride in the ingredients’ flavours, the chef shares a passion in common with the producers he relies on. A full range of pork cuisine is available, including cutlets and charcuterie. Ingredients are lightly salted to bring out their flavour, and portions are generous. The traditions of French food culture on full display.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都港区西麻布1-2-14 B1F

restaurants.name: Ma Poule
restaurants.description: The chef opened a small restaurant, Ma Poule to celebrate the appeal of Jura, in eastern France. This region, with its beautiful forests, valleys and wetlands and its wonderful ingredients and people, made a profound impression on the chef. Chicken and morels in sauce vin jaune is a Jura dish in which the chef takes great pride. This dish is paired with specially produced yellow wine. The façade and interior, and even proprietress’s clothing, are yellow as well. A restaurant filled with love of Jura.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都文京区西片2-19-17

restaurants.name: Madame Toki
restaurants.description: The Western-style brick house with red roof tiles catches the eye. Marble floors, antique furniture and chandeliers make it like visiting the villa of some European noble. The cuisine is simple and modern, scrupulously prepared and pleasing to the eye. A trolley arrives for you to choose your dessert, creating a moment of indulgence. The proprietress leads her excellent team of service staff.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都渋谷区鉢山町14-7

restaurants.name: Maeshiba Ryoriten
restaurants.description: The speciality is charcoal-grilled beef and sauce only. On this, the French cuisine of Taira Maeshiba is focused. Ingredients whose producers are listed by name, finely disciplined flame-tending techniques, and traditional yet distinctive sauces combine to form menus only Chef Maeshiba could compose. The fare is simple, the work meticulous.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都世田谷区玉川4-7-5 B1F

restaurants.name: Margotto e Baciare
restaurants.description: The restaurant’s name translates as ‘a whole kiss’. Truffles can be chosen not only whole, but also by the gram. The consommé, enjoyed in a large glass, offers the full, aromatic fragrance of truffles. The fried eggs on toast are accompanied by a rich truffle sauce. Both dishes showcase the synergy of fragrance and flavour created by truffles. Cuisine that proves that true value cannot be found in simply devouring truffles whole.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都港区西麻布4-2-6

restaurants.name: Matsunozushi
restaurants.description: Sushi items lined up inside a glass display case ooze Showa-era ambience. A sushi shop of the old school, and that’s how the chef likes it. Cutting and serving the sushi himself, the chef offers everything from omakase set menus to à la carte. Tuna is marinated in soy sauce; conger eel is coated in a thick, sweet eel sauce; and egg is served ‘kurakake’ or ‘saddle-style’: layered into a cake and split over a bite-sized mound of vinegared rice. The character for ‘sushi’ combines the characters for ‘fish’ and ‘delicious’. The pursuit of essential Edo style expresses the chef’s pride in his craft better than any words.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都豊島区南長崎2-16-12

restaurants.name: Meishan
restaurants.description: Two sisters run this Sichuan restaurant, the elder tending flame and wok in the kitchen, the younger serving guests plates of steaming fare. Stir-fried vegetables, served with pickled vegetables and fermented tofu, are home-style favourites. Steamed chicken and mapo tofu use spices to bring out the flavours of the ingredients. Framed Chinese paper-cutout art adorns the walls; the music of the erhu sets the mood. The reverence for Chinese culture and food is unmistakable.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都新宿区横寺町37-39

restaurants.name: Mejiro Shunkotei
restaurants.address: 2F, Trad Mejiro, 2-39-1 Mejiro, Toshima-ku, Tokyo, 171-0031, Japan
restaurants.description: ‘Novel yet nostalgic Western food’ is the theme here. Hamburger steaks with dollops of demi-glace sauce and gratin dressed in silky smooth béchamel sauce are prepared in time-honoured ways, bringing back the flavours of the good old days. Decompression-prepared salad offers rich flavours and uniquely fresh textures. Unchanging methods combine with advanced cooking techniques declare a new approach to Western favourites.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都豊島区目白2-39-1 2F, Trad Mejiro

restaurants.name: Mejiro Zorome
restaurants.description: Unagi are dressed, skewered and grilled over high-grade charcoal. From the counter seats, the whole series of cooking scenes can be observed. The unagi are kept alive in purifying tanks until they are dressed, then dipped three times in sauce and grilled Edo-style. Sauce is used sparingly, letting the flavour of the fish speak for itself. Rice cooked somewhat firm, and freshly ground sansho pepper deliver a distinctive touch. Skilful restraint shows in the short steaming time and sparing use of sauce.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都豊島区目白3-3-1 B1F

restaurants.name: Melograno
restaurants.description: ‘Melograno‘ is Italian for ‘pomegranate’, a symbol of the prosperity of one’s descendants. The name was given by the chef’s mentor back where he apprenticed, wishing for the success of his business. The chef himself is already training his successors, passing on further growth to the next generation. Fun and freedom are the qualities he seeks in his cooking. His potato-and-truffle tart takes inspiration from Italian desserts. A place to enjoy à la carte Italian cuisine inspired by Japanese ingredients.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都渋谷区広尾5-24-2

restaurants.name: Merachi
restaurants.description: The concept is a distinctly Japanese expression of Italian cuisine. Centred on recipes from southern Italy, the chef interprets them using Japanese ingredients and a free approach to technique. A year’s worth of tomato sauce is prepared using Tokyo-grown tomatoes, while mozzarella from Chiba offers a flavour unique to Japan. To expand his culinary repertoire, the chef has travelled to northern Europe and Asia. His breadth of experience brings greater refinement to regional Italian cooking.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都港区西麻布1-4-23

restaurants.name: Mitsui
restaurants.description: A wabi-inspired atmosphere, reminiscent of a traditional tearoom. A traditional cook stove mounted in the kitchen affords a view of the chef’s handiwork with charcoal brazier and broad-brimmed pot for cooking rice. The smoky scent of snacks from the brazier fills the air, while rice from the chef’s native Nagano cooks in the pot. Nigirizushi hews close to tradition, with white-fleshed fish, tuna, gizzard shad, shrimp, sea urchin and conger eel forming the core lineup. Three tuna pieces follow in succession—medium-fatty tuna seasoned in salt, soy-sauce marinated tuna, and rich fatty underbelly tuna—displaying a distinctive flair. Sushi rice enlivened with salt and vinegar keeps the sake going down smooth.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都港区麻布十番3-10-2 5F

restaurants.name: Miyasaka
restaurants.description: The framed motto reads, roughly, ‘Begin at the start, and when you get to the end, begin again with the spirit of the novice.’ Taking to heart this aphorism of tea master Sen no Rikyū, the chef toils at his craft with the sincerity of a beginner. He encountered this spirit in Kyoto, where he learned the art of chakaiseki. Rice is served the moment it is ready, so guests can observe the changes in it as the steam billows. Handmade sweets conclude the meal, following the etiquette of the wabicha style of the tea ceremony. Strong matcha also reflects the essence of the Way of Tea.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都港区南青山5-4-30

restaurants.name: Miyawaki
restaurants.description: This counter-style Japanese restaurant dips deeply into Kyoto culinary traditions. Kenta Miyawaki’s experience manifests in fish dishes grilled in soy sauce, sake and citrus slices, or with Saikyoni, served on Kyoyaki vessels. Deep-fried tofu mixed with thinly sliced vegetables is standard fare, preserving the work of previous generations. Ingredients, which include mollusc, unagi, mushrooms and lily bulbs, change with the season. First-time customers receive omakase set meals only; from their next visit they can order from an extensive menu.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都港区三田1-3-28

restaurants.name: Mochibuta Tonkatsu Taiyo
restaurants.description: The shop takes such pride in the quality of its pork—Mochibuta, a domestic pork known for its tender meat and light, clean-flavoured fat—that the brand is in the name of the restaurant. Pork is deep-fried in vegetable oil, on a low flame at a leisurely pace, for a light texture. The chef is a study in undivided concentration as he hovers over his pots. Tonjiru pork soup is generously full of ingredients. The location is next to Musashikoyama Shopping Street, famed for its long arcade. Reservations are accepted for evenings.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都品川区小山3-22-7

restaurants.name: Motoazabu Kushima
restaurants.description: Omakase set menus are woven from Japanese Black wagyu beef and seasonal cuisine. Homemade corned beef, shabu-shabu and char-grilled items are perennial favourites here, their flavours deftly highlighted by the chef’s kappo skills. The chef sources his beef from Miyazaki to showcase the attractions of his home region. To vary the pace and tone, mushrooms, puffer fish, crab and other seasonal delights are interspersed. A freewheeling menu brings both joy and satisfaction.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都港区元麻布3-11-2 B1F

restaurants.name: Mutsukari
restaurants.description: The chef leads his team of cooks like the lead goose guiding its flock in flight. Clad in red armbands and snow-white kitchen uniforms, the kitchen team embodies the spirit of Japan. The accent on vegetables echoes the chef’s experience in shojin-ryori, the vegetarian regimen of Buddhist monks. Mutsukari’s repertoire varies widely, from ohitashi, a boiled vegetable side dish, and nikogori, jellied broth of meat or fish, to various deep-fried and grilled foods. Sashimi is dressed with a vinegar and nori jelly. Casual variations make for delightful idiosyncrasies.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都中央区銀座5-5-19 6F

restaurants.name: Myojaku
restaurants.description: Hidetoshi Nakamura’s cuisine embodies harmony, subtlety, and purity. With minimal seasoning, he captures each ingredient’s subtle trace in water— using only water and sea salt. Mountain and sea are intimately linked through water. Nakamura reminds us that we are a part of the magnificent connection between Nature and her laws. This elegant aesthetic, of beauty through simplicity, exudes modesty, returning us to a spirit of gratitude for the natural world.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都港区西麻布3-2-34 B1F

restaurants.name: Myojinshita Soba Oshin
restaurants.description: The shop is a stone’s throw from Kanda Myojin Shrine, a Shinto shrine dedicated to the protection of Edo, or current-day Tokyo. The menu ranges from an impressive variety of appetisers to creative soba dishes incorporating ingredients such as raw tofu skin and dried mullet roe. For cold soba, you can choose between Seiro and Inaka; both are cut into thin noodles that go down smoothly. Milling and kneading methods are adjusted to the buckwheat variety to convey their distinct personalities. In the evening, you can order just a single plate of soba, allowing you to spend your time as you please.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都千代田区外神田2-8-2

restaurants.name: Mētis Roppongi
restaurants.description: Auspicious kumiko woodwork patterns, a counter lined with lacquered trays: the modern Japanese décor is in keeping with the culinary theme of ‘wakon yosai’ or ‘Japanese spirit with Western learning’. Cooking is mainly done over wood flame. Japanese seasons and ingredients, wrapped in French tradition and philosophy, all wreathed in the smoky aroma of firewood. Mētis is a goddess of wisdom in classical Greek mythology; perhaps she inspires the chef as he concentrates his own wisdom to weave delicious cuisine.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都港区六本木5-18-22

restaurants.name: NANAHIRO
restaurants.description: The concept is ‘Japanese cuisine plus’. The menu reflects the chef’s background in both Japanese and Western cooking, blending traditions to create something uniquely his own. Only the ingredients are listed, heightening anticipation and offering an element of surprise when the dishes arrive. The progression moves from seasonal vegetables, seafood and meat to freshly steamed rice and a delicate sweet course. More than Japanese, yet not quite Western, this is a place to savour premium ingredients with a refined touch.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都港区西麻布4-2-5 3F

restaurants.name: NARISAWA
restaurants.description: Narisawa celebrates the culture of Japan’s ‘satoyama’, rural hillscapes, through the genre of ‘innovative satoyama cuisine’. The Japanese islands form a long arc from north to south, and most of the land is satoyama. In this geographical backdrop from foothills to plains, people have found ways to live harmoniously and sustainably with nature. This image of the satoyama informs the imagination of Narisawa’s cuisine. The restaurant uses traditional craftworks such as lacquerware and washi paper, harmonising diverse people and culture.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都港区南青山2-6-15

restaurants.name: NISHIAZABU SHANGU
restaurants.description: The kitchen can be seen from the counter seating, making dining a theatrical experience. The chef commands his team while working his wok with consummate skill. Testifying that the appeal of Cantonese cuisine lies in seafood and dried items, the menu is laced with ingredients such as spiny lobster, abalone, dried scallop abductor and dried mushrooms. After dinner, guests can relocate to the lounge to bask in the afterglow of the performance. Desserts, a mix of Chinese and Western patisserie treats, close the evening.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都港区西麻布3-13-10 2F

restaurants.name: Nabeno-Ism
restaurants.description: Black, white and orange are the tricolore of ‘Watanabe-ism.’ Black represents faith that cannot be tainted; white, the freedom to become any colour; and orange, the flame manipulated by the chef. On offer here is a fusion of French and Edo food cultures. Sobagaki is emulsified using French techniques; local Japanese elements include kaminari-okoshi, a roasted and flavoured mochi cracker; and monaka, a sweet of azuki bean paste sandwiched between crisp wafers. Watanabe-ism has deep roots in Asakusa-Komagata.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都台東区駒形2-1-17

restaurants.name: Ne Quittez pas
restaurants.description: Toshio Tanabe was an Olympic gymnast candidate and a professional boxer before he became a chef, steering his ambitions for the wider world towards French cuisine. His billboard reads, ‘French cuisine with seasonal seafood and vegetables’. The secret stories behind the restaurant’s name, the fish soup and the grilled clams lay bare Tanabe’s mode of living. The ‘soil course’ and the watermelon prix fixe offerings are ideas from a unique imagination. Enjoy the flavours and share in the secrets.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都品川区東五反田3-15-19

restaurants.name: Nebuka
restaurants.description: Nebuka was opened by the proprietor of a wine bar. It is said that the best grapevines are those with the deepest roots (‘ne-buka’), and deep are the connections Nebuka cultivates with producers and in ingredients and cuisine. Omakase set menus focus on seasonal vegetables. Experienced in both French and Japanese cuisine, the chef applies expertise in both schools, and even creates pasta dishes. Each dish is arranged simply, placing the ingredients centre stage. A young team provides bright and pleasant service.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都港区六本木6-8-28 2F

restaurants.name: Negima
restaurants.description: Negima-nabe, a tuna and spring onion stew, is a tradition handed down since the days of the shoguns. At a time when lean meat was treasured, this stew was contrived as a way of lightening the texture of toro, the fatty cut of tuna. Enchanted with Edo-era cooking, the proprietress of Negima revived it. True to original recipes, the dish uses neither kombu nor mirin. The meal ends with ‘pepper rice’, a dish of fresh-cooked rice seasoned with broth from the pot and pepper. Sample the pure cooking that true Edo-ites have always loved.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都豊島区北大塚2-31-19 B1F

restaurants.name: NéMo
restaurants.description: As a boy, Chef Kenichi Nemoto was an enthusiastic angler, and seafood is the focus of the prix fixe menus he arranges. To ensure variety, Nemoto varies his preparation styles: frites, soups, butter roasting, and so on. In gratitude to the natural world of sea and mountain where he encounters his ingredients, he wastes nothing. The chef is earnest in his relations with producers, such as building bonds of trust with the fishermen of Shimoda.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都港区南青山6-15-4 B1F

restaurants.name: Night Market
restaurants.description: The very name of the restaurant builds excitement ahead of guest’s visit. The concept is a night market, which has roots in Southeast Asia. The chef, inspired by hawker cuisine experienced across multiple countries, interprets street-market dishes through Japanese ingredients. The menu is à la carte, the origin of each dish indicated by national flags. A visit here feels like a tour of the night markets of Vietnam, Thailand, Indonesia and Malaysia. Cuisines, cultures and people mingle in an exotic atmosphere.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都渋谷区渋谷2-6-6

restaurants.name: Nihombashi Sonoji
restaurants.description: The motto on the noren, ‘After having tempura, finish with soba’, declares the personality of Sonoji. The chef learned the arts of both soba and tempura when he ran a shop in his native Shizuoka. For tempura pieces, he focuses on the bounty of Shizuoka’s Suruga Bay and traditional Edo seafood. Vegetables shipped directly from farmers accentuate each season. The soba served at the end of the meal is topped with kakiage of sakura shrimp, ‘the jewel of Suruga Bay’. The unique menu reflects each stage on the chef’s gastronomic journey.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都中央区日本橋人形町2-22-11

restaurants.name: Nihonbashi Kawaguchi
restaurants.description: The restaurant is run by a chef trained at Sukiyabashi Jiro and a proprietress from the UK. Keen to share Tokyo’s culinary history, the pair have delved into Edo cuisine to shape their selection of appetisers. True to the chef’s apprenticeship, the nigiri progresses from lighter, more delicate fish to those of greater depth and richness. Three pieces of tuna are followed by gizzard shad, its bright acidity providing a crisp finish. The rice, distinctly seasoned with rice vinegar, is another legacy of the chef’s mentor. Inspired by Nihonbashi, the cradle of Edo food culture, the shop embraces a romantic vision of sushi’s origins.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都中央区日本橋本町4-5-13

restaurants.name: Nishiazabu Noguchi
restaurants.description: Honest Japanese cuisine, overlaid with inspirations from the chef. Keen to ensure everyone’s satisfaction, Nishiazabu Noguchi offers an extensive menu. The delicate broth in soup dishes gets its flavour from kombu. Tsukuri are dressed in salt water flavoured with sudachi to bring out the fragrance of the seafood. For the main event, white rice, takikomi-gohan (rice with a variety of ingredients) and unadon (rice topped with eel) are all served, enhancing satisfaction. Devotion to the spirit of service shines in the generous free rice refills.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都港区西麻布1-10-16

restaurants.name: Nishiazabu Otake
restaurants.description: The chef practises ‘cuisine that lingers in the soul’ that he learned at a ryotei, or traditional high-end Japanese restaurant, in Gifu Prefecture. Croquettes, a perennial favourite, are as seasonal as they are whimsical, stuffed with hair crab in spring and porcini mushrooms in autumn. Char-grilled fare includes meat as well as fish, enhancing satisfaction. Uniquely, Nishiazabu Otake serves game such as winter duck and Asian black bear, sent by hunters in Gifu. Definitely Japanese cuisine, yet free of convention.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都港区西麻布1-4-23

restaurants.name: Nishiazabu Sushi Shin
restaurants.description: The movements are those of one immersed in the world of nigiri. On the fingertips of the left hand balances a piece of fish; clutched in the right, a fistful of sushi rice; the tip of the forefinger applies wasabi. Pushing, occasionally pulling, and scoring in delicate strokes, the single-edged knife crafts hidden incisions to help flavour soak in and heat travel through. Gracefully flowing motion, fine-tuned through daily practice, is the simplicity that holds countless truths. The spirit of nigirizushi is in capable hands with Shintaro Suzuki.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都港区西麻布4-18-20

restaurants.name: Nishiazabu Taku
restaurants.description: Back when there weren’t many sushi restaurants in Nishi-Azabu, the chef was determined to blaze a trail here. And ‘blaze a trail’ is basically what Taku means. The same trailblazing was what led him to be among the first sushi chefs to hire a sommelier, and to spread sushi culture in Hawaii. The variety of his omakase set meals, which start at 30 items, is eye-popping. Nigiri items alternate with drinking snacks. Sushi rice may be seasoned with white or red vinegar, depending on the fish topping—another trailblazing move.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都港区西麻布2-11-5

restaurants.name: No Code
restaurants.description: Not bound by existing frameworks, setting no limits, embracing a free style and rejecting categorisation: these are the ideas behind No Code. Guided by this philosophy, the cuisine draws on Mexican, French and Japanese influences. The omakase course combines ingredients, techniques and flavours from all three. Through food, established culinary codes are reimagined for today. Part of the pleasure lies in that freedom.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都港区西麻布2-25-31 2F

restaurants.name: Noda
restaurants.description: The chef’s culinary journey began with reading a cookbook called ‘Memos on Cooking’. He mixes and matches Japanese and Western ingredients and cooking styles, creating novel flavours. Filtering through his experience in French cuisine, he layers ingredients to create new culinary expressions. In a nod to modern sensibilities, pairings are recommended for marriage with each dish. The roots lie in ‘terroir’ and hometown cooking, in both France and Japan.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都渋谷区神宮前6-9-9

restaurants.name: Nodaiwa Azabu Iikura Honten
restaurants.description: This restaurant has been in business since the days of the Edo shogunate. Kanejiro Kanemoto is its fifth-generation proprietor, committed to a life of craftsmanship. Guided by the belief that eel is ‘a delicacy for the people,’ he has worked to share its appeal. With practiced craftsmanship, he patiently steams the eel to drip out excess fat then grills it without seasoning in the shirayaki style, or dips it in sauce and broils it to create beguilingly aromatic kabayaki. The dipping sauce has evolved over the generations, with the proportions of soy sauce and mirin shifting according to changing tastes. Kanemoto trains fresh cohorts of cooks, keeping the eel culture of Edo alive.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都港区東麻布1-5-4

restaurants.name: Nogizaka Shin
restaurants.description: The look of the establishment is a ‘modern-day tearoom’, with concrete walls and glass-enclosed kitchen. The operation is run by the proprietor and the sommelier, both of whom have experience working in Japanese restaurants in Paris. From the proprietor’s native Tokushima come supplies of seafood, citrus fruits and rice. Another Tokushima influence is the use of Awa bancha, a fermented tea. Nogizaka Shin hosts tasting events every month, featuring discussions about pairings with wines and Japanese sake. The teamwork between chef and sommelier points to the future of kappo.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都港区赤坂8-11-19

restaurants.name: Nogizaka yui
restaurants.description: The chef is passionate about ‘the ties that bind’. He chose the name ‘yui’, meaning ‘ties’ or ‘connections’, to mark his restaurant as a place that links guests with food producers; people with people. The gracious service by the warm-hearted proprietress also conveys that spirit. Cuisine based on Sichuan traditions combines with vegetables delivered directly from farms. Nogizaka yui connects with Japan’s seasons as well, evidenced by sour soup of bamboo shoots and beef in spring and soy-sauce stir fry of organic maitake mushrooms in autumn. Seasoning is restrained to place ingredients centre stage.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都港区南青山1-23-12

restaurants.name: Noto Kanazawanosachi Ginza Furuta
restaurants.description: The couple in charge of this restaurant want to tell the world about the wealth of food from Ishikawa Prefecture. Fish arriving from Nanao, the proprietress’ place of birth, is prepared by her husband, the owner-chef. In addition to vinegared mozuku seaweed and rosy sea perch cooked whole, the restaurant serves mountain treats such as vegetables from Kaga and edible wild plants from the Noto Peninsula. The couple tour farms and sake breweries, building warm relations with producers.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都中央区銀座5-10-11

restaurants.name: Noyashichi
restaurants.description: The restaurant explores a fusion of Japanese and Chinese cuisine. An apprenticeship in Shanghai led the chef to reflect on how to present a distinctly Japanese expression of Chinese cooking. Inside, flowers adorn the Japanese décor and burning incense gently scents the space. The cuisine features clear soup enriched with kombu kelp and sashimi paired with chilli oil, arranged on Japanese tableware. Trading ideas with masters of Japanese cuisine, the chef fine-tunes his individual style, expressing a spirit of harmony between Japanese and Chinese culinary cultures.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都新宿区荒木町2-9

restaurants.name: NÉN TOKYO
restaurants.description: A modern-style Vietnamese restaurant has its first Japanese branch. ‘NÉN’ is a wild aromatic herb native to central Vietnam, reflecting a desire to introduce lesser-known aspects of the country’s cuisine. The set menus draw inspiration from nature—sea, mountains and rivers. National staples such as pho and banh mi reinterpreted through a distinctive lens. Each dish has a story, introduced with an accompanying hand-drawn card.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都渋谷区代官山町14-18 4F

restaurants.name: Nœud. TOKYO
restaurants.description: The focus of the cuisine is aroma above all. Dishes are built around vegetables, with parts such as leaves, stalks and peels transformed through a variety of cooking methods. Presentation is modern, a layering of flavours and textures. The restaurant’s approach of ‘All Sustainable French’, using everything, wasting nothing, is its way of discovering new facets of familiar ingredients. Recycled materials in the décor reflect the same conviction.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都千代田区平河町2-5-7 B1F

restaurants.name: O2
restaurants.description: ‘O2’ (‘oh-two’) is a play on Chef Otsu’s name. Like his bow tie and denim apron, the pun is an Otsu trademark. Presented as Chinese cuisine, the fare at O2 really defies easy categories, making use of both Western and Japanese ingredients. The chef’s code is to use Chinese elements in both technique and seasoning. The playful interior also expresses Otsu’s unique vision.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都江東区三好2-15-12

restaurants.name: Oku
restaurants.description: Asakusa is a second hometown for the chef, who has lived here since his apprenticeship. He inherited both spirit and skill, along with tools and serving ware, from his mentor. He reveres the old teachings of the sushi world but does add a few twists of his own. He adds sweet potato shochu to rich soy syrup; to rolled omelette, he adds soy milk. The character for the chef’s surname of ‘Oku’ developed from a combination of the characters for ‘house’, ‘rice’ and ‘palm of hand’; a sign, he contends, that he was destined to run a place where he would fashion rice meals with his hands.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都台東区浅草3-42-11

restaurants.name: Onigiri Asakusa Yadoroku
restaurants.description: This family has been serving onigiri in Asakusa for three generations. Ingredients are arrayed inside a glass case, as at a sushi shop, to be formed into onigiri on the spot. Offerings range from salmon and dried plum to rarer items such as herring roe pickled in sake lees and opossum shrimp preserved and simmered in sweetened soy sauce. Offering a wide range of ingredients allows customers to choose based on their physical condition; the area was once home to numerous bars and pubs, so customers would drop in for morsels to soothe constitutions punished by a night on the town.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都台東区浅草3-9-10

restaurants.name: Oniku Karyu
restaurants.description: The chef’s love of beef began when he was a boy, when his father would cook thick-cut steaks for him. He is a beef evangelist, proclaiming its appeal to the world. His decision to create a ‘meat kappo’ arose from his training in kaiseki. Wagyu beef is used in every Japanese preparation style, including nigiri sushi, char-grilled and shabu-shabu. The beauty of the traditional building, the kimono-clad service and the colourful dinnerware all add to the ambience. Through beef cuisine, Oniku Karyu is spreading the word about Japanese culture.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都中央区銀座1-14-6 7F

restaurants.name: Orangutan
restaurants.description: A Thai restaurant that showcases the cuisine of the ancient city of Chiang Mai and the Isaan region in northeastern Thailand. The menu includes a salad of pounded green papaya, fermented pork sausages, and a soup of stewed beef and herbs. The local style is to eat these with sticky rice steamed in a bamboo basket. The sour and spicy flavours of fermented seasonings, along with the aromas of citrus and fresh herbs, evoke a Thai dinner table.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都中野区中野5-53-4

restaurants.name: Orchestra
restaurants.description: Imola, the city in Italy’s Emilia-Romagna where the chef apprenticed, has a famous music academy. The restaurant’s name derives from the passion for classical music he learned from the students he befriended there. In Imola, the chef developed his belief that a restaurant resembles an orchestra, in that a wide range of players work in harmony to create a greater whole. Cuisine on plates decorated with musical instrument motifs and classical music piped into the dining room deliver a performance worthy of any conservatory.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都渋谷区代々木4-1-7

restaurants.name: Oryori Horiuchi
restaurants.description: The starting point for Mrs. Sayaka Horiuchi is her early life lived amid natural beauty. Considering that ‘the mountains water the village and feed the ocean,’ the dishes are prepared with a spirit of gratitude for ingredients from land and sea. Horiuchi nurtures relations with food producers nationwide who share her vision. Local vegetables are from farmers in Nerima, clams from fishermen in Kyoto. Her famous chicken offal stew is home cooking from her native Yamanashi Prefecture.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都新宿区荒木町9-15

restaurants.name: Oryori Ichiho
restaurants.description: The name is a play on ‘ho’, which could mean ‘step’, as in ‘stepping forward’ or ‘bounty, prosperity’, reflecting chef Ryusuke Yamazaki’s desire that his restaurant blossom and prosper. The soup stock gets its umami from kombu, with a light flavour reflecting his Kyoto experience. Dishes such as fish steamed with grated turnip on top and dumplings made from bamboo-shoot derive from ages past, recounting the culinary culture of the ancient capital. Pike conger in summer and eddoes in winter are eagerly awaited.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都渋谷区恵比寿1-23-10 3F

restaurants.name: Oryori Katsushi
restaurants.description: The owner-chef’s obsession with dashi soup stock was learnt from his mentor, a Kansai native. He mixes dashi of dried bonito flakes, kombu kelp and clams to impart depth and richness. This dashi is found throughout: in savoury egg custard, soup dishes, hot pots and other Japanese favourites. The chef’s choice of career is down to the influence of his brother, a sushi chef, and this brotherly bond is shown in his vinegared rice, simmered abalone liver sauce and pressed chub mackerel sushi. The menu is a stage on a fascinating journey that we’re going to keep watching.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都中央区銀座8-7-19 3F

restaurants.name: Oryori Kokoroba
restaurants.description: The name incorporates a character from the chef’s name while professing sincere devotion to gastronomy. That sincerity is on full display in the hassun appetisers. To convey the mood of the passing seasons, the chef incorporates meticulously chosen items fresh from the market. Oyakodon (chicken and egg on rice) made with freshly cooked rice is a sentimental favourite of the chef, one he’s made continuously since his apprenticeship. His dedication shines through in his painstaking attention to his work.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都中央区日本橋人形町2-10-11 3F

restaurants.name: Oryori Tsuji
restaurants.description: A cypress counter in a room with adze-hewn ceilings and clay walls – an austere décor by which the proprietor sets the tone. ‘Simple fare’, says the menu, whose offerings avoid shows of splendour in favour of honest presentation. Sense of season flows entirely from the taste and aroma of the food. Sashimi wrapped in kombu, for example, or wanmono prepared with clear soup. Results are all the more impressive for the effort paid where few will notice. Natural flavours to calm the soul.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都港区東麻布3-3-9 B1F

restaurants.name: Osobano Kouga
restaurants.description: Taste sake and snacks while dining on soba. Think of the culture of the soba shop, the beloved essence of Edo popular culture, plunked down near Nishi-Azabu Intersection for the modern generation to enjoy, and you have the atmosphere of Osobano Kouga. Evenings bring prix fixe presentations that encourage you to linger over cups of sake. Tensui, tempura soba with the soba removed, is an old-timey snack. Soba in season and appetisers of vegetable or fish of the day are a delight.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都港区西麻布2-14-5

restaurants.name: Osteria da K. [káppa]
restaurants.description: An osteria presented by a sushi shop. After apprenticing in Italy, the chef helped out at a sushi restaurant before taking charge here. Seafood, procured from the chef’s personal sources, catches the eye. Cut ends of abalone and fish make a seafood ragù for pasta, born from meals prepared for employees. Acqua pazza is enhanced with kombu water for an even more delicious dish. Cuisine that brings out the best in the ingredients, backed by the skills of Italy and Japan.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都中央区銀座8-7-2 5F

restaurants.name: Otora
restaurants.description: A guiding principle of the chef’s cuisine is ‘junkan’ — a philosophy that views the seasons in ten-day spans. Within each span, ingredients reach their peak expression. Each dish is at its finest in the very moment it is prepared and served. The chef cherishes every fleeting instant that, once gone, cannot return, and remains grateful for the human connections formed each day. Traditionally, a month is divided into ‘jojun’, the first ten days; ‘chujun’, the middle ten days; and ‘gejun’, the final ten days. Each span offers a precious encounter with the season and its finest ingredients from land and sea.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都港区西麻布4-5-4

restaurants.name: PER TE
restaurants.description: Pizza and other items are offered with this prix fixe menu of wood-fired cooking. The Marinara and Margherita pursue the essence of these classics, while more creative pizzas draw inspiration from regional Italian cuisine, exploring new possibilities. Fresh from the oven, pizzas are served one slice at a time, ensuring both portion and pacing are balanced. The course unfolds in a chic setting, offering a new vision of the pizzeria.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都港区赤坂2-12-33

restaurants.name: PONTE DEL PIATTO
restaurants.description: ‘Ponte’ means ‘bridge’, while ‘piatto’ means ‘dish’. The earnest desire of the chef is to build bridges among people through food. Prix fixe menus express the seasons of Japan, but cooking methods and flavours are Italian through and through. Ribollita is a vegetable soup from Tuscany. Tiramisu is raised to a new level. Beautiful, modern arrangements attest to the chef’s many years of honing his craft.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都渋谷区広尾5-19-7

restaurants.name: PRIMO PASSO
restaurants.description: The name ‘Primo Passo’ means ‘first step’. The chef was in charge of pasta at the celebrated Quattro Passi restaurant in Naples before taking his first step towards independence in Shintomicho. His aim is to create ‘Italian cuisine with a Japanese twist’. He uses plenty of dashi and Japanese ingredients. Also incorporating Japanese elements are the coffered ceiling, mud walls and vertically written menu. He serves several small dishes featuring his speciality pasta to showcase a variety of flavours.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都中央区築地1-5-11 B1F

restaurants.name: PRISMA
restaurants.description: Standing alone in his kitchen, Tomofumi Saito engages with Italian cuisine in earnest. His attention to detail is relentless, from the cuisine itself to the music, flowers and other elements. Seating is limited to ensure each dish gets his undivided attention. When he creates new menu items, there are no prototypes: he pushes himself hard to create inventive cuisine found nowhere else. 'Prisma' means 'prism' in Italian and the high standards Saito demands of himself shine in a rainbow of culinary inventiveness.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都港区南青山6-4-6

restaurants.name: PRUNIER
restaurants.address: 2F, Tokyo Kaikan, 3-2-1 Marunouchi, Chiyoda-ku, Tokyo, 100-0005, Japan
restaurants.description: PRUNIER is the main dining room of Tokyo Kaikan, a meeting-place cherished for generations. The décor conjures images of fish and flowing water, harking back to the restaurant’s earliest days when it specialised in seafood. Here you can enjoy modern French cuisine that reflects the shifting seasons, presented in an elegant setting. Sole bonne femme, handed down from generations of head chefs, keeps a tradition alive and well. Gracious service adds an extra touch to the meal.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都千代田区丸の内3-2-1 2F, Tokyo Kaikan

restaurants.name: Patous
restaurants.description: Bread finishes baking the moment guests arrive. The warmth and aroma of freshly baked bread are the chef’s way of graciously welcoming you. ‘Pan-seared layered scabbard fish, potato and foie gras’ has been the speciality since the restaurant’s inception. The chef’s experience comes to the fore in his subtle manipulation of flame and texture. Patous is a common name for Great Pyrenees, a breed of dog. The white fur of this dog reflects the restaurant’s pure devotion to its craft.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都港区麻布台3-4-14

restaurants.name: Piao-Xiang
restaurants.description: With ‘Old Sichuan’ as its guiding principle, the restaurant reinterprets and refines the flavours of Sichuan’s past. Each course draws inspiration from Chinese history and its notable figures. Dish names are written in two kanji characters, inviting guests to imagine their flavours. ‘Furong’ takes its cue from the hibiscus, the official flower of Chengdu, while ‘Guifei’ incorporates the wine favoured by the famed Tang-era beauty Yang Guifei. The chef’s passion is thoughtfully conveyed through the manager’s explanations.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都渋谷区広尾5-19-1

restaurants.name: Piatto Mitsu
restaurants.description: Chef Mitsuaki Okamura opened his own place in Fukuoka, but love of Azabu drew him back here. He sources his ingredients from Kyushu, owing to the strong relationships he has built with producers there. The shop’s speciality is abalone, served deep-fried, oven-baked or in pasta. Hometown dishes are all the more impressive for their simple presentation. ‘Piatto’ is Italian for ‘plate’, while ‘Mitsu’ is part of Chef Mitsuaki Okamura’s name, hence the name suggests ‘Mitsu’s plates’; his own style of cooking.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都港区元麻布1-7-12 7F

restaurants.name: Plaiga TOKYO
restaurants.address: M2F, Nihonseimei Marunouchi Garden Tower, 1-1-3 Marunouchi, Chiyoda-ku, Tokyo, 100-0005, Japan
restaurants.description: The concept is ‘French cuisine on a journey through Japan’s four seasons.’ Seasonal ingredients are treated with French techniques to create a delicate cuisine. Dishes of vegetables that don’t fit Japan’s strict standards for shape and colour are served on vessels reimagined from discarded glass. The purpose of this sustainable odyssey, considerate of both food and environment, is to point to a future of new perspectives and destinations in which harmony with the environment is celebrated.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都千代田区丸の内1-1-3 M2F, Nihonseimei Marunouchi Garden Tower

restaurants.name: Pont d'Or Inno
restaurants.description: The name ‘Pont d’Or’, literally ‘Golden Bridge’, is a wish that French cuisine and the district of Nihombashi will both prosper. Tasteful décor, reminiscent of a European house, oozes panache. Chef Ken Yuhara incorporates Japanese elements into classic French cuisine taught to him by Noboru Inoue. Manager Toru Ozaki regales guests with explanations of each dish, his attentive service adding to the meal like a floral bouquet. A golden bridge anchored by three talented partners.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都中央区日本橋室町2-4-3 1F, YUITO

restaurants.name: Ponta Honke
restaurants.description: Since its inception in 1905, this Western restaurant has been run by four generations of the same family. The chef’s famous cutlet is deep-fried in lard carefully trimmed from the pork he will fry. Everything is done by hand, as it has been from the first generation. The youthful energy and practiced skill of the chef is evident in every detail, even down to the hand-chopped cabbage. Traditional flavours and skills testify to his mettle. Delicious fare and the gentle ambience of a family enterprise make everyone feel at home.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都台東区上野3-23-3

restaurants.name: Principio
restaurants.description: Principio is Italian for ‘beginning’, and the meal begins with a greeting from the cheerful couple that makes you feel right at home. The chef’s specialities are his hand-rolled pasta and charcoal-grilled delicacies. To evoke a sense of journeying, prix fixe menus feature pasta dishes from different regions of Italy. Charcoal-grilled aged pork and shorthorn beef are seasoned simply with salt and pepper. The intimate scale of the restaurant keeps the couple in close proximity, fostering a sense of comfortable familiarity.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都港区麻布十番2-4-8 2F

restaurants.name: Quatre Vingt Douze
restaurants.description: French cuisine overlays ingredients of varying flavours and textures to express a medley of tastes. That defining characteristic, says the chef, is the wellspring of his creative drive and the key to his approach in assembling his menu. Fruits, herbs and spices are all deployed to good effect and with a light touch to create his flavours. Fish and meat dishes always come with sauces, paying homage to the classics. The proprietress is in charge of the desserts. An intimate eatery from a couple who support each other like a synchronized team.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都世田谷区船橋1-9-5 2F

restaurants.name: Quintessence
restaurants.address: 1F, Garden City Shinagawa Gotenyama, 6-7-29 Kitashinagawa, Shinagawa-ku, Tokyo, 141-0001, Japan
restaurants.description: The name comes from Shuzo Kishida’s passion for discovering the true essence of French cuisine. The chef pursues his quest through three processes: ingredients, flame and seasoning. For Kishida, ‘ingredients’ means deep respect for food producers, common to all aspects of cooking; ‘flame’ means flame-handling that is finely attuned to the fish or meat at hand; and ‘seasoning’ is flavouring that is tailored to each ingredient. The soul of cooking lies in this trinity. The white spaces on the menu leave space for your imagination.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都品川区北品川6-7-29 1F, Garden City Shinagawa Gotenyama

restaurants.name: RAMEN MATSUI
restaurants.description: The husband boils the noodles; the wife garnishes them with spring onion and fermented bamboo shoots. Between them, they bring alpha and omega together to complete each bowl of ramen. Flavours are based on soy sauce, salt and dried sardines. To bring out the aroma, pure rice sake is added to chicken or seafood dashi soup stock. Several types of fragrant oil are used, deepening the flavour experience. Kombu kelp, scallops and wheat flour, all from Hokkaido, infuse the husband’s love of his homeland.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都新宿区四谷4-25-10

restaurants.name: REI
restaurants.description: The concept is ‘neo-machichuka’, ‘machichuka’ denoting an unpretentious neighbourhood Chinese restaurant and ‘neo’ referencing an upgrade for modern tastes. A culinary tune-up, so to speak, with the accent on changing aromas and textures. Red sea-bream salad, for example, is dressed with scorched rice for a smoky fragrance. Mouth-watering chicken, prepared in black vinegar to deepen the flavour, accentuates red pepper and nutty texture. Menu items are reassuringly familiar, yet each recipe harbours a novel twist.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都渋谷区元代々木町10-8

restaurants.name: REQUINQUER
restaurants.description: Soichi Furuya seeks out the attractions and possibilities of French cuisine while staying in touch with current trends. Terrine de foie gras is a speciality that keeps customers coming back. Soups and crepes made using vegetables that don’t meet JA size regulations support farmers. The various set menus focused on the unique traits of Japanese-French fusion cuisine excite diners. The restaurant’s name means ‘be of good cheer’, and indeed REQUINQUER invigorates diners and food producers alike.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都港区白金台5-17-11

restaurants.name: RISTORANTE Al Porto
restaurants.description: Mamoru Kataoka is a pioneer who has broadened the appeal of Italian gastronomy in Japan. His passion for seafood stems was inspired by the seafood restaurants he encountered on a visit to Milan in the 1960s. Al Porto’s famous pasta is a seafood ragù prepared with lashings of clams and shrimp. Kataoka manages the kitchen and restaurant with his son. When time allows, father and son circulate among the tables, ever mindful of the importance of graceful service.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都港区西麻布3-24-9 B1F

restaurants.name: ROPPONGI RIAN
restaurants.description: The concept of this kappo is ‘as much as you want of what you want’. Seasonal fruits and vegetables are lined up on the counter, ready to be cooked by your preferred method. The head chef honed his craft in Kyoto, so he uses rice and water from Kyotango. Pike conger and oysters are sourced from Nagasaki, reflecting his Amakusa roots. The champon served to close out the meal is a recipe from his mother, who runs a shop specialising in this noodle dish.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都港区六本木6-2-6 4F

restaurants.name: ROZZO SICILIA
restaurants.description: The largest island in the Mediterranean, Sicily has flourished for centuries as a hub of trade, so the cuisines of many lands have been passed down here. The unique local dishes that sprang up on this island captured the heart of ROZZO SICILIA’s chef. Authentic recipes are valued, including aubergine caponata, fennel and sardine pasta and seafood couscous. Lemon as an essential accompaniment is another traditional touch. The chef’s cherished goal is to craft flavours that remind Sicilians of home.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都港区白金1-1-12

restaurants.name: Raisan
restaurants.description: The name derives from the masterwork of French epicure Jean Anthelme Brillat-Savarin, The Physiology of Taste, translated as Bimi Raisan. Raisan’s owner was deeply impressed by how timeless gastronomy is. His guiding purpose is to serve dishes light on salt that diners won’t weary of eating. Each dish uses a particular type of dashi stock, including shrimp, dried fish and shellfish. Raisan means ‘praise’, and we praise the warm spirit pervades the service here.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都渋谷区代々木5-9-9

restaurants.name: Ramen Break Beats
restaurants.description: The chef was a DJ in a former life, and the technique he applied in that calling, break beats, informs both the name of the restaurant and the music piped through the dining room. The signature dish is soy-sauce ramen, which owes its beautiful presentation and clear soup to careful preparation of the noodles. The purity of the broth delights the palate from the first mouthful. With rhythmic tempo, you’ll reel in the noodles and drink every drop of soup before you know it. A beat-driven bowl that hit big in Meguro.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都目黒区目黒4-21-19

restaurants.name: Ramenya Toy Box
restaurants.description: The chef’s starting point is the ramen his grandmother cooked for him when he was a child. He added ‘Toy Box’ to the name because he wanted to create ramen that holds the same excitement for his guests as a box of toys does for a child. Soy sauce ramen, the shop’s flagship offering, is constantly tinkered with to improve it. In search of a depth of gustatory experience, over 10 types of soy sauce are combined in the broth. Yet the ramen is a dish of simple happiness; noodles from the good old days.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都荒川区東日暮里1-1-3

restaurants.name: Regalo
restaurants.description: ‘Regalo’ is Italian for ‘gift’. Chef Tomomi Ogura uses Japanese ingredients such as bamboo shoots, sweetfish and Pacific saury to bring the seasons of Japan to his guests. Combinations of ingredients are to be kept to a minimum; set menus should offer variety . These two principles summarise cucina Ogura, which literally means ‘Ogura’s Kitchen’. A calculated minimalism creates precisely the desired effect.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都渋谷区代々木4-6-2 B1F

restaurants.name: Ren Mishina
restaurants.description: True to his creed, ‘Life resides in cooking that is free of affectation’, the chef strives to serve food that is honest and down-to-earth. Paying close attention to the strength of his cooking flame, he sources the flavours of each season—bamboo shoots, pike conger, matsutake mushrooms, and puffer fish—and slow-cooks them over charcoal. Indian-ink paintings behind the counter depict lotuses in each season. In the language of flowers, the lotus represents a pure and chaste spirit. The chef’s honesty and respect for foodstuffs are reflected in his carefully pared-down creations.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都中央区銀座7-3-13 B1F

restaurants.name: Ristorante ACQUA PAZZA
restaurants.address: 2F, Passage Aoyama, 2-27-18 Minamiaoyama, Minato-ku, Tokyo, 107-0062, Japan
restaurants.description: Chef Yoshimi Hidaka has broadened the horizons of Neapolitan hometown cooking. Acqua pazza, the poached white fish dish that so thrilled him in Naples, is both the restaurant’s name and its signature dish. Prepared by fishermen, it began as fish and tomatoes simmered in seawater but here freshwater is used and Manila clams are added for saltiness. The synergy of umami from inosinic acid in the fish, glutamic acid in the tomato and succinic acid in the Manila clams is classic Yoshimi Hidaka ingenuity.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都港区南青山2-27-18 2F, Passage Aoyama

restaurants.name: Ristorante Angelo
restaurants.description: The restaurant's name, meaning ‘angel’, was inspired by an Osaka eatery whose influence guides the chef each day. He sources his ingredients from within the Kansai area with which he is familiar. Sea bream from Akashi is aged, daggertooth pike conger from Awaji served as fritters. Game is served here too, including venison and boar from Tamba-Sasayama and duck from Wakayama. White porcelain dinnerware provides a canvas for modern arrangements, innovative enough at times to impress as art.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都品川区東五反田3-17-21

restaurants.name: Ristorante HONDA
restaurants.description: Seasonal foodstuffs are the theme at this modern Italian restaurant. Tetsuya Honda’s culinary creations link the traditional cuisine of Italy with the seasons of Japan. The speciality, ‘Sea Urchin Tagliolini’, uses no cream, relying instead on lashings of sea urchin to achieve a rich, satisfying flavour. Honda strives to elicit the flavour of each ingredient.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都港区北青山2-12-35

restaurants.name: RyuGin
restaurants.address: 7F, Tokyo Midtown Hibiya, 1-1-2 Yurakucho, Chiyoda-ku, Tokyo, 100-0006, Japan
restaurants.description: Seiji Yamamoto charts the vastness of Japanese cuisine. He handles his knives and tends his charcoal grill with relentlessly honed technique. He elucidates the properties of each ingredient and the best ways to prepare it with a scientific eye, displaying unwavering resolve. His winter menu is replete with fugu items, products of years of experience and passion for Japanese cuisine. Disarming frankness shines in Yamamoto’s favourite expression: ‘I’m a chef because I love cooking.’ From January to March, RyuGin offers only the exclusive fugu course.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都千代田区有楽町1-1-2 7F, Tokyo Midtown Hibiya

restaurants.name: Ryuen
restaurants.description: The name means ‘swallows in the willow trees’ and it encapsulates the hope that guests will come back to Ryuen just as the swallows return to the willows each spring. The chef’s apprenticeship taught him reverence for ingredients; he learned the skill of using everything in food preparation, wasting nothing. Beef dishes are served as a middle course, taking after Oniku Karyu, Ryuen’s sister restaurant. Each dish is inventive while preserving the traditions of Japanese cuisine. The chef’s quest for new flavours embodies the spirit of onko-chishin: discovering the new by studying the past.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都中央区日本橋人形町2-10-11 7F

restaurants.name: Ryuzu
restaurants.description: The crown, ‘ryuzu’ in Japanese, is a vital part of a watch. Under the leadership of Ryuta Iizuka, the team moves like clockwork: chefs, patissiers, servers, and the sommelier working in perfect synchrony, like the gears of a precision timepiece. Foodstuffs from the chef’s native Niigata Prefecture, as well as vegetables from the Noto region of Ishikawa Prefecture, reflect the essence of each passing seasons. The passion of food producers is transformed into cuisine by the kitchen and conveyed to guests through graceful service.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都港区六本木4-2-35 B1F

restaurants.name: SAKAKI
restaurants.description: The prix fixe menu offers a wide selection, making the choice a pleasurable struggle. The emphasis on seafood reflects the chef’s experience in southern France. Sea urchin with consommé jelly is a signature dish, while seasonal fare provides variety with each visit. On weekday lunchtimes, the restaurant operates as a Western-style eatery, drawing queues before opening for its famous pork ginger. Firmly footed in Kyobashi, it remains lively throughout the day and into the evening.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都中央区京橋2-12-12

restaurants.name: SANTOSHAM
restaurants.description: The cuisine of Kerala, a state on India’s southwest coast, takes the limelight here. The owner-couple, along with a chef from Kerala, are consummate ambassadors for the appeal of this culinary discipline. The sweetness of coconut, the pungency of curry leaves and the flavour of black pepper are standout features. Kerala is a coastal state, so seafood features prominently. Santosham means ‘happiness’ in the Malayalam language; the name is a prayer that good food and happiness will go hand in hand here.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都千代田区神田小川町3-2 2F

restaurants.name: SIGNATURE
restaurants.address: 37F, Mandarin Oriental Tokyo, 2-1-1 Nihombashimuromachi, Chuo-ku, Tokyo, 103-8328, Japan
restaurants.description: Pâté en croûte, fish bouillabaisse, lamb baked in pie crust: SIGNATURE breathes modern elements into recipes interwoven with French history. The chef reveres French sauces, while using herbs and vinegar for a light finish. From the dining room, decorated in blue throughout, the view is exhilarating. The service team’s pleasant demeanour also puts the mind at ease.

restaurants.name: SUKIYAKI ASAI
restaurants.description: Experience sukiyaki hospitality at a counter. Standing right across from you, the server expertly prepares alternating slices of beef and vegetables. Sourced from a distinguished butcher in Shiga Prefecture, the choice beef is served in a variety of thicknesses. On the side are the signature dipping sauces: meringue-like beaten egg and rich egg yolk mixed with Tosa vinegar. The meal culminates with a final dish of shirataki noodles, stir-fried in warishita sauce and beef fat. Each dish will enchant both your eyes and taste buds.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都港区虎ノ門3-11-15

restaurants.name: Sakuragi
restaurants.description: The ‘counter kappo’ format was adopted to serve food as fresh as possible from the kitchen. Dried bonito is shaved and dashi is made for the soup right before your eyes. Fish is char-grilled and roasted on straw. Keeping a watchful eye over his kitchen, the chef serves each dish at just the right temperature and with an alluring aroma. Fresh-cooked rice can be savoured topped with raw egg or seasoned minced chicken. Though presentations range widely, each one conveys a desire to satisfy and delight guests.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都中央区築地3-7-2 2F

restaurants.name: Sanosushi
restaurants.description: The chef’s path was inspired by the meticulous craftsmanship of sushi masters. Hearkening back to Edo-period food culture, he strives for orthodoxy, committed to generously sized nigiri with thick-sliced toppings. Simmered clam and anago are finished with a rich nitsume sauce. A former interior contractor, the chef designed the restaurant himself. Traditional elements such as the tsukedai, wooden trays used for serving sushi, and handwritten fish names on the wall evoke a sense of nostalgia.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都港区芝2-18-9

restaurants.name: Sanwa
restaurants.description: ‘Sanwa’ means ‘three-part harmony’: between producers, chefs and guests. The menu lists producers by name, linking them to guests through the chef. Prix fixe service begins with dry-cured ham and gnocco fritto. Meat dishes include venison and beef, both char-grilled. The meal ends with a pasta dish, as per convention. Sanwa exerts special effort to make each dish impressive, so the number of main ingredients is limited to three—a three-part harmony on the plate.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都港区白金台5-13-14 B1F

restaurants.name: Sasaki Seimenjo
restaurants.description: The chef ’s aim is to create a ramen shop beloved by locals. He sources ingredients from greengrocers and butchers in Nishiogi, treasuring his interactions with local people. The distance from the train station is also a blessing, as neighbourhood schoolchildren and their families can be seen sitting shoulder-to-shoulder, happily slurping noodles. As befits the ‘Seimenjo’, or ‘noodle-maker’, in the name, his use of Japanese wheat is a selling point. In addition to standard thin noodles, he also offers flat noodles. The miso flavour is a much-anticipated treat in autumn and winter.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都杉並区西荻北4-26-10

restaurants.name: Sassa
restaurants.description: Kaiseki prepared with a sushi chef’s sensibility. Abalone risotto is a luxury, prepared with generous lashings of boiled abalone. In an original interpretation, tuna is sliced thin, to complement the light soy sauce. Rice is prepared a sushi chef’s way, cooked with a minimum of water to bring out the flavour when eaten. Pu-erh tea is served after dinner, reflecting the chef’s culinary experience in Shanghai. Bursting with passion and ambition to innovate, Sassa is moving Japanese cuisine forward.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都渋谷区広尾5-13-6

restaurants.name: Saucer
restaurants.description: The name is French: saucer used as a verb, to pour sauce or to drizzle sauce on bread. The standard fare is what the chef terms ‘saucer’: freshly baked bread on one plate and a sauce on the other. Consommé drawn over a period of three days is a key ingredient. Morel mushrooms in spring and sweetfish in summer impart seasonal flavours. As an apprentice, the chef was appointed saucier, sauce master; his confidence shines in his work today.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都渋谷区恵比寿西2-7-10 B1F

restaurants.name: Sazenka
restaurants.description: Tomoya Kawada sees cooking as a reconciliation between nature and humanity. He names his approach Sazenka, which literally means ‘tea-Zen-Chinese’, out of a desire to create Chinese fare that mediates between the worlds of tea and Zen. His cuisine harmoniously combines the techniques he learned in China with the soul and spiritual features of Japan – ‘wakon-kansai’ is the theme. Japanese ingredients in season are used in spring-roll fillings and Sichuan stir-fries, lending a seasonal dimension to Chinese cuisine.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都港区南麻布4-7-5

restaurants.name: Seiju
restaurants.description: The chef learned his tempura skills at Rakutei, a now-closed Akasaka restaurant. Oil is replaced frequently, paying careful attention to its temperature, the humidity and the nature of the ingredients used. The chef recalls how the series of movements by which tempura pieces are coated in batter and deep-fried was seared into his memory as he watched his mentor, a true craftsman, at work. The flow of the meal is distinctly old-school Tokyo, yet the chef knows how to make his tempura unique with a few novelties, such as mochi rice cake with dried mullet roe.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都中央区築地3-16-9 B1F

restaurants.name: Seisoka
restaurants.description: The name means ‘a tranquil space surrounded by lush greenery’. It conveys the richness of Japanese culture, which cherishes the beauty of the changing seasons. Featuring vibrant vegetable and seafood delicacies, the menu reflects the emotions of each season in both presentation and tableware. By serving shojin ryori, the vegetarian cuisine developed by Zen monks, the restaurant aims to preserve ancient culinary tradition. It values its connection to the Zen-sect Tengenji Temple, as it is located in the grounds.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都港区南麻布4-2-34

restaurants.name: Seizan
restaurants.description: The name combines two of the four characters of Haruhiko Yamamoto’s name. The chef’s approach to cuisine and commitment to entertaining guests were both learned from his mentor in Gifu. He is also enthusiastic about guiding the next generation, as shown by the smiles and positive attitude of his young crew. Yamamoto may surprise with innovative dishes, but with wansashi—the pairing of sashimi and soup, considered the essence of Japanese cuisine—he stays true to tradition. Each item is the product of painstaking effort the customer never sees.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都港区三田2-17-29 B1F

restaurants.name: Seki Hanare
restaurants.description: Creative dining focused on affinity with sake. Appetisers are served as a combination platter, the better to keep the libations flowing. Tsukuri feature ingenious arrangements of condiments. Uniquely, meat dishes are an indispensable part. Each dish is generous, making every morsel an unforgettable memory. The collection of sake and shochu, amassed on visits to breweries, is impressive; as the shop specialises in local tipples, the wine and whisky selection is also strictly Japanese.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都世田谷区世田谷3-1-3

restaurants.name: Sennomago
restaurants.description: The chef devotes himself to good health through a balanced diet, using Chinese medicines as seasoning to serve food that is salutary for both mind and body. Vegetables from his native Oita are grown by natural methods, making them gentle to the constitution. At dinner, there is a wide range of à la carte dishes, leaning toward Sichuan and Shanghai influences. The chef allows himself an individual take with the mapo tofu: drawing on a wealth of experience, he offers a choice of Sichuan, aged doubanjiang (Chinese broad bean chili paste) and salt. Lunchtime set menus are also popular.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都杉並区西荻北4-4-2

restaurants.name: Sense
restaurants.address: 37F, Mandarin Oriental Tokyo, 2-1-1 Nihombashimuromachi, Chuo-ku, Tokyo, 103-8328, Japan
restaurants.description: Perched high above the Mandarin Oriental Tokyo, Sense commands sweeping views that immerse you in the heart of the city. While honouring Cantonese tradition, the chef also seeks a more contemporary flavour. Experience with Hong Kong’s food culture is delicately conveyed through Japanese ingredients. Lunch on weekends and public holidays is also a chance to enjoy dim sum. Shumai and dumplings prepared by the dim sum chef are beautiful to look at too.

restaurants.name: Series
restaurants.description: Service consists of many dishes in small quantities, hence the name. Creativity abounds, inspired by ingredients and preparation methods from numerous countries. Steamed spicy beef rather than chicken, chicken-wings stuffed with foie gras, and Peking duck wrapped in kadaif pastry show off Series’ talent for arrangement. To accentuate the variety of culinary styles, pairings range widely as well. Pair wine, sake or a cocktail with your meal.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都港区麻布台3-4-11

restaurants.name: Sharikimon Onozawa
restaurants.description: Makoto Onozawa finds inventive ways to break the mould while respecting tradition. Soup dishes and sashimi uphold the basics, reassuring the diner. Broiled unagi seasoned in soy-based sweet sauce is served together with broiled unseasoned unagi for a special treat. Rolled sushi of tuna and pickled daikon radish are served in the intervals, bringing satisfaction. The meal concludes on two comfort-food notes: soba and curry. Breaking tradition, after all, is about seeing the job to completion.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都新宿区荒木町6-39

restaurants.name: Shigeyuki
restaurants.description: ‘The look surprises, the taste reassures.’ The chef’s passion for creativity makes his culinary spirit unique. Decoratively arranged sashimi are briefly heated to draw out moisture. The chef doesn’t serve soup dishes, as much of his cooking tailors the dashi stock to each dish. His freewheeling personality makes the evening fun, while the folk-art vibe of the interior, including a chest of drawers inherited from the chef’s grandparents, fills the air with memories and the warmth of family.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都渋谷区西原2-17-3

restaurants.name: Shimbashi Sasada
restaurants.description: With his expertise refined at a Kyoto cuisine restaurant, the chef emphasises seasonal ingredients, showcasing his experience in dishes that highlight their finest qualities. He starts with delicacies and aemono appetisers designed to enhance the enjoyment of sake. The warm boiled wild mustard greens with deep-fried tofu is a Sasada speciality perfected over many years. All the dishes are carefully prepared with a focus on simplicity, showcasing the chef’s humble personality and dedication to his craft.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都港区西新橋1-23-7

restaurants.name: Shin Harada
restaurants.description: Italian dining by Shinji Harada, a chef who prizes aroma. The elegant dining room is a refreshing white. As you’re seated, you’ll notice how high the table is – the purpose is to draw the aroma of the food that much closer. Shinji Harada’s cherished theory of ‘shortest distance to the ingredients’ means not only bringing out the flavour of domestic ingredients but also bringing face and plate closer together.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都中央区銀座2-6-5 12F

restaurants.name: Shinjiko Shijimi Chukasoba Kohaku
restaurants.description: After taking over his mother’s restaurant, the owner-chef opened his doors in the town where he was born and raised. The key to the flavour lies in the shijimi clams from Lake Shinji in Shimane Prefecture, after which the restaurant is named. The soup utilizes the umami of the shellfish’s succinic acid. To enjoy the true taste of the broth, we recommend the salt-flavoured option. The names of the fishermen who caught each day's clams are written on the blackboard, showing respect for both ingredients and producers.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都大田区西六郷2-1-3

restaurants.name: ShinoiS
restaurants.description: The chef, once dedicated solely to Cantonese cuisine, expanded his repertoire as he honed his craft in Hong Kong and Shanghai. Prix fixe menus incorporate traditions from regions across China, using ingredients selected from a broad purview. The chef focuses not only on seasoning but also on the natural flavours of the ingredients; dried abalone simmered in water alone testifies to his conviction. After dinner the chef serves Chinese tea, conveying gratitude to guests as he converses with them.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都港区白金台4-2-7 2F

restaurants.name: Shinrakuki
restaurants.description: ‘Hong Kong cuisine and organic wine’ sums up the concept. We recommend ordering Cantonese flame-broiled items to start. Meat is suspended over an open hearth for patient roasting. On the grill, meat is skewered or hung from special hooks and cooked over direct flame. Pork cuts and duck are sweetened with honey or malt sugar and seasoned with soy sauce or Chinese five-spice powder, which condense the umami as the meat broils. The steamed whole grouper and the pigeon pickled in tamari soy sauce are offered by advance reservation only.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都新宿区若葉2-7

restaurants.name: Shintomicho Yuasa
restaurants.description: The character for ‘food’ (食) is composed of the components for ‘person’ (人) and ‘good’ (良), signifying the power of food to make a person good. Daisuke Yuasa, who puts great store by the principles of a balanced diet and good food for good health, creates dishes he hopes will support his patrons’ health in both body and soul. Another of his wishes is that food should be fun, so he gained a sommelier qualification to help customers expand their enjoyment.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都中央区新富2-7-4

restaurants.name: Shiomachi
restaurants.description: ‘Shiomachi’ means waiting for the right tide to push out to sea. The name expresses the owner-chef’s wish to create a shop where people congregate, just as fishermen gather to await favourable conditions. Yearning for the sea, the chef was drawn to a job where he handles fish. He visits Toyosu Market daily, ever on the lookout for that once-in-a-lifetime catch. He chats with brokers as he makes purchases and drains fish of blood to preserve freshness. Gather here to enjoy your fill of high-quality fish.
restaurants.addressJa: 東京都渋谷区上原1-34-10 2F

restaurants.name: Shiosoba Jiku
restaurants.description: The chef heard the call to the culinary life one day while riding his father’s motorcycle on a long tour around the Seto Inland Sea. When he happened upon a ramen shop along the way, the taste of the salty soba touched his soul. He combines a clear seafood broth with homemade noodles crafted with aroma in mind. The ceiling decoration recalls the roof of an old-time food stall, honouring how ramen culture spread in his native Hiroshima Prefecture.
